result:
The rain doesn't fall here anymore. It used to. I remember the sound of it on the tin roof of my grandmother's porch, like a thousand tiny fingers drumming out a code I never learned to read. Now the sky is just this pale, exhausted thing that hangs over everything like a used sheet. The dust gets in your teeth. You learn to like the taste.

I keep thinking about the whale they found in the middle of Nebraska in 2029. Not a fossil. A real one. Still wet. Scientists lost their minds. Religious people lost their shit in the other direction. Me? I just felt this weird relief. Like finally the world had admitted it was broken in a way that couldn't be ignored. The whale had barnacles on it. Actual ocean barnacles. It had been swimming somewhere, minding its own business, and then physics got drunk and vomited it into a cornfield two thousand miles from any sea that still existed.

My therapist says I fixate on impossible things because I can't face the possible ones. She's probably right. The possible ones are all bills and my mother's cancer and the way my ex still likes my Instagram stories even though she lives with someone named Kyle now. Kyle. Jesus.

Sometimes I drive out to where the whale landed. There's nothing there anymore. Just a plaque and some dead grass that refuses to grow back. The locals turned the whole thing into a tourist trap before the whale even stopped smelling. They sold "Whaleburgers" (beef, obviously) and t-shirts that said I GOT WHALED IN NEBRASKA. Then the water wars started and nobody cared about miracles anymore. Miracles don't fill reservoirs.

I have this theory that reality is getting thinner. Like an old pair of jeans. You can see the white threads underneath. Every once in a while something from the other side pushes through. A whale. A child in India speaking perfect ancient Greek for three days then forgetting everything. Those birds in China that all flew backwards for six minutes in 2031. The scientists call it "quantum leakage." I call it the universe having a stroke.

My grandmother used to say that God speaks in coincidences until you ignore Him long enough, then He starts shouting in tragedies. I don't believe in God but I believe in whatever's doing the shouting. It's getting louder.

Last night I dreamed I was the whale. Not inside the whale. I was the whale. The pressure was enormous but familiar. Like wearing a suit made of the entire ocean. I could feel every current, every temperature change, every bioluminescent flicker for hundreds of miles. It was the least lonely feeling I've ever had. Then the dream shifted and I was falling upward, out of the water and into the sky, and the sky had corn in it, and I was screaming but whales don't have voices that work in air.

I woke up tasting salt.

The thing is, I think we're all whales sometimes. Swimming through something so vast we can't see the edges. Thinking we're in control because we have these sophisticated navigation systems. Then one day physics gets bored and drops us somewhere that doesn't make sense. And we just lie there gasping, wondering what the hell happened to the water.

My mother called today. Her voice sounded like paper. She said the new medication makes everything taste like pennies. I told her that's just her body remembering what blood tastes like. She laughed. It was a small sound but it had weight. Like a whale's heart. They have the biggest hearts in the world, you know. Fourteen hundred pounds. A kid could crawl inside one.

I want to crawl inside something that big. Not to hide. Just to feel what it's like to be that undeniably here. That impossible to ignore.

The rain isn't coming back. Not the way it was. But sometimes at night I hear it anyway. That old code on the tin roof. Maybe it's not rain. Maybe it's something trying to get through. Maybe it's the universe practicing its shouting in a gentler register first.

I hope it is.

I really hope it is.
